title: Merck — HIV — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: Merck HIV — Doravirine/Islatravir (IDVYNSO, Pifeltro, Delstrigo)

Products: IDVYNSO (doravirine/islatravir), PIFELTRO (doravirine), DELSTRIGO (doravirine/lamivudine/TDF) Manufacturer: Merck & Co. (MSD) Indication: HIV-1 infection in adults — treatment-experienced (switch) and treatment-naive Document scope: Product-level competitive/clinical landscape Last updated: April 24, 2026

1. Disease Landscape

1.1 Epidemiology

Approximately 1.2 million people in the United States are living with HIV, with ~35,000-40,000 new diagnoses annually. The treatment landscape has evolved into a maintenance paradigm — the vast majority of diagnosed PLWH are on antiretroviral therapy (ART), and the clinical goal has shifted from viral suppression to long-term tolerability, simplification, and quality of life:

  • ~1.2M PLWH in the US — ~75% on ART, ~66% virologically suppressed
  • Treatment-experienced/suppressed: The largest commercial opportunity. Most PLWH on ART are virologically suppressed and eligible for regimen simplification or switch
  • Treatment-naive: ~35,000-40,000 new diagnoses/year — smaller market but strategically important for building prescribing habits
  • Key HCP segments: Infectious Disease specialists (IDs), Primary Care Physicians (PCPs), NP/PAs — IDs manage the most complex cases; PCPs increasingly manage stable, suppressed patients

1.2 Drug Classes in HIV Treatment

  • NRTIs (Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors): Backbone of most regimens (tenofovir, emtricitabine, lamivudine, abacavir)
  • INSTIs (Integrase Strand Transfer Inhibitors): Current standard-of-care anchor class (dolutegravir, bictegravir, cabotegravir). Dominate guidelines
  • NNRTIs (Non-Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors): Doravirine is the only modern NNRTI with competitive tolerability; older NNRTIs (efavirenz, rilpivirine) largely displaced by INSTIs
  • NRTTIs (Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Translocation Inhibitors): Novel class — islatravir is the first-in-class NRTTI, with a unique mechanism of action (translocation inhibition + chain termination)
  • Protease Inhibitors (PIs): Largely relegated to salvage therapy
  • Long-acting injectables: Cabenuva (cabotegravir + rilpivirine, monthly/bimonthly IM injection) — growing share in patients who prefer injection over daily oral

1.3 Standard Treatment Algorithm

Newly diagnosed HIV-1 patient
  |
  v
ART initiation (as soon as possible, often same-day "rapid start")
  |
  ├── Preferred 1L regimens (DHHS Guidelines):
  |     ├── Biktarvy (BIC/FTC/TAF) — #1 prescribed, INSTI-based 3-drug STR
  |     ├── Dovato (DTG/3TC) — 2-drug INSTI-based STR
  |     ├── Triumeq (DTG/ABC/3TC) — INSTI-based 3-drug STR
  |     └── DOR/ISL (IDVYNSO) — first non-INSTI 2-drug STR (newly approved)
  |
  ├── Alternative 1L regimens:
  |     ├── Pifeltro + Descovy or Truvada (doravirine + NRTI backbone)
  |     ├── Delstrigo (doravirine/3TC/TDF) — Merck's 3-drug STR
  |     └── Cabenuva (injectable, for patients preferring non-oral)
  |
  └── Suppressed patients — Switch options:
        ├── Biktarvy (most common switch-to)
        ├── Dovato (2-drug simplification)
        ├── IDVYNSO (non-INSTI, tenofovir-free 2-drug switch) ← NEW
        └── Cabenuva (injectable switch)

1.4 Unmet Needs

  1. INSTI-sparing options: INSTIs dominate, but a subset of PLWH experience weight gain, neuropsychiatric effects, or prefer alternatives. No competitive non-INSTI option existed until IDVYNSO
  2. Tenofovir-free regimens: Tenofovir (TDF and TAF) is associated with renal and bone concerns in long-term use. Eliminating tenofovir while maintaining efficacy is clinically meaningful
  3. Weight neutrality: INSTI-based regimens (especially dolutegravir) are associated with weight gain. Doravirine has a favorable weight profile
  4. Lipid-friendly regimens: PLWH have elevated cardiovascular risk; doravirine demonstrates a superior lipid profile vs protease inhibitors and comparable to INSTIs
  5. Drug-drug interaction simplicity: Doravirine has fewer DDIs than PIs and some INSTIs, relevant for aging PLWH with polypharmacy
  6. Long-acting options: Growing demand for injectables (Cabenuva, lenacapavir), but many patients still prefer oral daily dosing — the oral simplification play is distinct from the injectable play

2. Product Profiles

2.1 IDVYNSO (doravirine 100mg / islatravir 0.25mg) — Merck

Brand name: IDVYNSO FDA approval: April 21, 2026 Availability: Pharmacies from May 11, 2026 Class: NNRTI (doravirine) + NRTTI (islatravir) — first-in-class combination Approved indication: Treatment of HIV-1 in adults who are virologically suppressed (HIV-1 RNA <50 copies/mL) on a stable ART regimen, with no history of virologic treatment failure and no known resistance substitutions associated with doravirine

Key clinical data:

  • Phase 3 MK-8591A-051/052/053: Non-inferior efficacy to BIC/FTC/TAF (Biktarvy) at Week 48 in both treatment-naive and virologically suppressed adults
  • Week 96 data (CROI 2026): Maintained virologic suppression in switch patients
  • First non-INSTI, tenofovir-free, once-daily complete 2-drug regimen with head-to-head Phase 3 data vs Biktarvy

Differentiators:

  • Non-INSTI anchor (appeals to INSTI-intolerant or INSTI-averse patients)
  • Tenofovir-free (no renal/bone concerns)
  • 2-drug regimen (fewer components = fewer potential toxicities)
  • Novel MOA (islatravir NRTTI — high barrier to resistance, unique mechanism)
  • Favorable weight and lipid profile

Limitations:

  • Switch-only label initially (treatment-naive NDA expected later, based on CROI 2026 data)
  • Contraindicated with strong CYP3A inducers and with lamivudine/emtricitabine co-administration
  • No long-acting formulation (oral daily only)
  • Limited real-world data at launch
  • Islatravir's earlier clinical setback (CD4 decline at higher doses in PrEP) may create perception headwinds

Revenue projection: ~$1.8B by 2033 (7MM, analyst consensus)

2.2 PIFELTRO (doravirine 100mg) — Merck

FDA approval: August 2018 Class: NNRTI Indication: Treatment of HIV-1 in adults with no prior ART history OR virologically suppressed adults, used in combination with other ARVs

Positioning: Add-on/backbone NNRTI for patients who need an INSTI-sparing regimen. Positioned for PLWH with weight concerns, comorbidities (dyslipidemia, renal impairment), or those on complex multi-drug regimens needing flexibility

ZoomRx SFE data (Aug-Dec 2025):

  • Share of Voice: 28% (vs Gilead 42%, ViiV 30%)
  • Merck reps reach ~65% of HCPs monthly, 3rd behind Gilead and ViiV
  • Top recalled messages: Superior lipid profile (PIF10, 73%), flexibility with ARVs (PIF9, 67%), indicated in combo (PIF11, 65%)
  • Most effective messages: No dose adjustment/renal (PIF3, 62% ME), flexibility (PIF9, 58% ME)
  • Rep closing improved to 52% (up from prior wave), on par with Dovato, above Biktarvy
  • ~40% of HCPs had improved PIFELTRO perceptions post-interaction

2.3 DELSTRIGO (doravirine/lamivudine/TDF) — Merck

FDA approval: August 2018 Class: NNRTI + 2 NRTIs (3-drug STR) Positioning: Complete regimen for suppressed patients; being superseded by IDVYNSO as the preferred Merck STR

ZoomRx SFE data: Tracked alongside Pifeltro in multi-product interactions; top message recall covers superior lipid profile (DEL7), appropriate patient types for switch (DEL3), weight change data (DEL8)

3. Competitive Landscape

3.1 Biktarvy (bictegravir/emtricitabine/TAF) — Gilead Sciences

The dominant franchise. Biktarvy generated ~$13B+ globally in 2024, commands ~45-50% share of new prescriptions and switching. DHHS-preferred regimen. 90% sampling penetration per ZoomRx data.

Strengths:

  • 5+ years of real-world data, zero resistance in 9 Phase 3 trials
  • DHHS guideline-recommended for rapid initiation (only INSTI-based STR with this recommendation)
  • Physician comfort and familiarity — the "TAGRISSO of HIV"
  • Gilead's dominant SOV (42%) and highest reach (~80%+ HCPs/month)
  • Message effectiveness: BIK8 (rapid initiation, 83% ME) and BIK3 (resistance, 81% ME) are the highest-rated messages in the study

Vulnerabilities:

  • Weight gain signal (INSTI class effect)
  • Tenofovir (TAF) — renal/bone concerns for long-term use
  • 3-drug regimen (more components than 2-drug alternatives)
  • Patent cliff concerns (bictegravir composition patent expiry timeline)

3.2 Dovato (dolutegravir/lamivudine) — ViiV Healthcare (GSK)

The 2-drug INSTI alternative. Dovato is IDVYNSO's most direct competitor — both are 2-drug STRs targeting simplification.

Strengths:

  • Proven 2-drug regimen concept; non-inferior to 3-drug regimens at 144 weeks
  • PASO DOBLE 96-week data (Oct 2025): matched Biktarvy efficacy with significantly less weight gain
  • Strong efficacy messaging: DOV1 (viral suppression with fewer medicines) has 80% ME — highest of any message in the study
  • ViiV's HIV-focused sales force and deep KOL relationships

Vulnerabilities:

  • INSTI-based (same weight gain class concern, though less than Biktarvy per PASO DOBLE)
  • Lamivudine backbone (lower resistance barrier than TAF-based regimens)
  • Dolutegravir patent expiry 2028 (generic competition imminent)

3.3 Cabenuva (cabotegravir + rilpivirine injection) — ViiV Healthcare

The injectable disruptor. Monthly or bimonthly IM injection for virologically suppressed adults.

Strengths:

  • First long-acting injectable ART — appeals to patients who dislike daily pills
  • Growing rapidly; projected to represent ~1/3 of GSK's HIV revenue by 2026
  • Strong patient satisfaction and adherence

Vulnerabilities:

  • Injection site reactions, requires clinic visits
  • INSTI-based (cabotegravir)
  • Not for all patients — oral-preferring patients remain the majority
  • Patent expiry in US ~2026

3.4 Lenacapavir (Sunlenca) — Gilead Sciences

The next frontier. First-in-class capsid inhibitor. Currently approved for heavily treatment-experienced patients. Being developed as twice-yearly subcutaneous injection for both treatment and PrEP.

Strengths:

  • Twice-yearly dosing is transformative if broadly approved
  • Novel MOA with no cross-resistance to existing classes
  • Gilead's PrEP franchise extension

Threat to Merck: If lenacapavir gains broad treatment approval, it could redefine convenience beyond what daily oral regimens offer. However, it's injectable — a different competitive space from oral simplification.

3.5 Islatravir + Lenacapavir — Merck/Gilead collaboration

Pipeline: Phase 3 trials (ISLEND-1, ISLEND-2) evaluating oral once-weekly islatravir + lenacapavir. This represents Merck's bet on the long-acting oral space and a strategic collaboration with its primary competitor.

4. Merck's HIV Strategy

4.1 Strategic Position

Merck is the #3 player in HIV behind Gilead and ViiV, with a small but growing franchise:

  • Current portfolio: Pifeltro ($144M peak forecast) and Delstrigo ($369M peak forecast) — combined sub-$500M, not blockbusters
  • IDVYNSO launch (May 2026): Transformative moment — first product with head-to-head Phase 3 data vs Biktarvy, first non-INSTI 2-drug regimen
  • Pipeline: DOR/ISL treatment-naive NDA expected (Phase 3 data from CROI 2026 supports filing), islatravir + lenacapavir (once-weekly oral), islatravir + ulonivirine (MK-8507)
  • Stated commercial opportunity: $15B by mid-2030s (per Merck investor presentation, June 2025)

4.2 Commercial Dynamics (from ZoomRx Studies)

Sales force activity (Aug-Dec 2025):

  • Merck reps reach ~65% of HCPs monthly, consistently 3rd behind Gilead (~80%) and ViiV (~70%)
  • Share of voice: Merck 28%, Gilead 42%, ViiV 30%
  • Activity consistent across IDs vs PCPs, but lower among NP/PAs vs MDs
  • Average interaction duration: 16.3 minutes (longest in market, above Gilead 15.9, ViiV 14.1)

Messaging strategy:

  • PIFELTRO messages focus on flexibility/dosing (PIF3, PIF9) and lipid profile (PIF10) — well-prioritized, top recalled messages are also most effective
  • Patient type focus: PLWH with weight concerns, comorbidities, dyslipidemia, renal impairment — niche but defensible positioning
  • Competitor contrast: Dovato focuses on efficacy, Biktarvy on legacy/durability — Merck occupies the tolerability/flexibility lane

Rep execution:

  • General improvement in rep performance this wave across all attributes
  • iPad/visual aid usage (86% of interactions) is high and correlates with better message recall and product perceptions
  • Rep closing improved to 52% (vs 41% Biktarvy, on par with 52% Dovato)
  • Key opportunity: increase NP/PA reach where Gilead/ViiV are stronger

Sampling strategy (July 2025 study):

  • 55% of HCPs prefer sales rep delivery vs 45% mail delivery
  • Target list HCPs prefer rep delivery 17% more (64% vs 47% off-target)
  • HIV Specialists prefer rep delivery 12% more (61% vs 49% PCPs)
  • Biktarvy dominates sampling (90% penetration) with primarily rep delivery
  • Recommendation for DOR/ISL: Prioritize sales rep delivery, consider hybrid model for high-volume/PCP practices

4.3 Transition from Pifeltro/Delstrigo to IDVYNSO

The 2026 SFE planning document confirms:

  • DOR/ISL (IDVYNSO) expected approved late April 2026, promotions beginning May 1, 2026
  • Merck reps will promote ONLY DOR/ISL (no more PIFELTRO promotions)
  • ZoomRx tracking: 2 waves in 2H 2026 (N=90 unique HCPs per wave), tracking DOR/ISL vs Dovato vs Biktarvy
  • Study setup post-approval, with 4-6 week buffer before fielding
  • Wave 1 topline: week of July 27; full report: week of Sep 7
  • Wave 2 topline: week of Nov 23; full report: week of Jan 11, 2027

5. Key Questions for Vinoth

  1. Will IDVYNSO's switch-only label limit uptake? The treatment-naive data exists (CROI 2026) but isn't in the label yet. How fast can Merck expand the indication?
  2. Can Merck's #3 sales force compete with Gilead's dominant presence? IDVYNSO needs to win share from Biktarvy — that means winning HCP time against the market leader's 42% SOV
  3. Is the non-INSTI positioning broad enough? The "INSTI-sparing" niche may be too small unless Merck can reframe it as "modern simplification" beyond just INSTI-averse patients
  4. How does lenacapavir (twice-yearly injectable) change the oral calculus? If long-acting injectables take share from all orals, IDVYNSO's window narrows
  5. What does the ZoomRx DOR/ISL SFE study need to capture? First wave tracking starts mid-2026 — this is the first measurement of IDVYNSO's commercial execution

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

1. Disease Landscape
1.1 Epidemiology
Approximately 1.2 million people in the United States are living with HIV, with ~35,000-40,000 new diagnoses annually. The treatment landscape has evolved into a maintenance paradigm — the vast majority of diagnosed PLWH are on antiretroviral therapy (ART), and the clinical goal has shifted from viral suppression to long-term tolerability, simplification, and quality of life:

~1.2M PLWH in the US — ~75% on ART, ~66% virologically suppressed
Treatment-experienced/suppressed: The largest commercial opportunity. Most PLWH on ART are virologically suppressed and eligible for regimen simplification or switch
Treatment-naive: ~35,000-40,000 new diagnoses/year — smaller market but strategically important for building prescribing habits
Key HCP segments: Infectious Disease specialists (IDs), Primary Care Physicians (PCPs), NP/PAs — IDs manage the most complex cases; PCPs increasingly manage stable, suppressed patients

1.2 Drug Classes in HIV Treatment

NRTIs (Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors): Backbone of most regimens (tenofovir, emtricitabine, lamivudine, abacavir)
INSTIs (Integrase Strand Transfer Inhibitors): Current standard-of-care anchor class (dolutegravir, bictegravir, cabotegravir). Dominate guidelines
NNRTIs (Non-Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors): Doravirine is the only modern NNRTI with competitive tolerability; older NNRTIs (efavirenz, rilpivirine) largely displaced by INSTIs
NRTTIs (Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Translocation Inhibitors): Novel class — islatravir is the first-in-class NRTTI, with a unique mechanism of action (translocation inhibition + chain termination)
Protease Inhibitors (PIs): Largely relegated to salvage therapy
Long-acting injectables: Cabenuva (cabotegravir + rilpivirine, monthly/bimonthly IM injection) — growing share in patients who prefer injection over daily oral

1.3 Standard Treatment Algorithm
Newly diagnosed HIV-1 patient
  |
  v
ART initiation (as soon as possible, often same-day "rapid start")
  |
  ├── Preferred 1L regimens (DHHS Guidelines):
  |     ├── Biktarvy (BIC/FTC/TAF) — #1 prescribed, INSTI-based 3-drug STR
  |     ├── Dovato (DTG/3TC) — 2-drug INSTI-based STR
  |     ├── Triumeq (DTG/ABC/3TC) — INSTI-based 3-drug STR
  |     └── DOR/ISL (IDVYNSO) — first non-INSTI 2-drug STR (newly approved)
  |
  ├── Alternative 1L regimens:
  |     ├── Pifeltro + Descovy or Truvada (doravirine + NRTI backbone)
  |     ├── Delstrigo (doravirine/3TC/TDF) — Merck's 3-drug STR
  |     └── Cabenuva (injectable, for patients preferring non-oral)
  |
  └── Suppressed patients — Switch options:
        ├── Biktarvy (most common switch-to)
        ├── Dovato (2-drug simplification)
        ├── IDVYNSO (non-INSTI, tenofovir-free 2-drug switch) ← NEW
        └── Cabenuva (injectable switch)

1.4 Unmet Needs

INSTI-sparing options: INSTIs dominate, but a subset of PLWH experience weight gain, neuropsychiatric effects, or prefer alternatives. No competitive non-INSTI option existed until IDVYNSO
Tenofovir-free regimens: Tenofovir (TDF and TAF) is associated with renal and bone concerns in long-term use. Eliminating tenofovir while maintaining efficacy is clinically meaningful
Weight neutrality: INSTI-based regimens (especially dolutegravir) are associated with weight gain. Doravirine has a favorable weight profile

Drug-drug interaction simplicity: Doravirine has fewer DDIs than PIs and some INSTIs, relevant for aging PLWH with polypharmacy
Long-acting options: Growing demand for injectables (Cabenuva, lenacapavir), but many patients still prefer oral daily dosing — the oral simplification play is distinct from the injectable play


2. Product Profiles
2.1 IDVYNSO (doravirine 100mg / islatravir 0.25mg) — Merck
Brand name: IDVYNSO
FDA approval: April 21, 2026
Availability: Pharmacies from May 11, 2026
Class: NNRTI (doravirine) + NRTTI (islatravir) — first-in-class combination
Approved indication: Treatment of HIV-1 in adults who are virologically suppressed (HIV-1 RNA <50 copies/mL) on a stable ART regimen, with no history of virologic treatment failure and no known resistance substitutions associated with doravirine
Key clinical data:

Phase 3 MK-8591A-051/052/053: Non-inferior efficacy to BIC/FTC/TAF (Biktarvy) at Week 48 in both treatment-naive and virologically suppressed adults
Week 96 data (CROI 2026): Maintained virologic suppression in switch patients
First non-INSTI, tenofovir-free, once-daily complete 2-drug regimen with head-to-head Phase 3 data vs Biktarvy

Differentiators:

Non-INSTI anchor (appeals to INSTI-intolerant or INSTI-averse patients)
Tenofovir-free (no renal/bone concerns)
2-drug regimen (fewer components = fewer potential toxicities)
Novel MOA (islatravir NRTTI — high barrier to resistance, unique mechanism)
Favorable weight and lipid profile

Limitations:

Switch-only label initially (treatment-naive NDA expected later, based on CROI 2026 data)
Contraindicated with strong CYP3A inducers and with lamivudine/emtricitabine co-administration
No long-acting formulation (oral daily only)
Limited real-world data at launch
Islatravir's earlier clinical setback (CD4 decline at higher doses in PrEP) may create perception headwinds

Revenue projection: ~$1.8B by 2033 (7MM, analyst consensus)
2.2 PIFELTRO (doravirine 100mg) — Merck
FDA approval: August 2018
Class: NNRTI
Indication: Treatment of HIV-1 in adults with no prior ART history OR virologically suppressed adults, used in combination with other ARVs
Positioning: Add-on/backbone NNRTI for patients who need an INSTI-sparing regimen. Positioned for PLWH with weight concerns, comorbidities (dyslipidemia, renal impairment), or those on complex multi-drug regimens needing flexibility
ZoomRx SFE data (Aug-Dec 2025):

Share of Voice: 28% (vs Gilead 42%, ViiV 30%)
Merck reps reach ~65% of HCPs monthly, 3rd behind Gilead and ViiV
Top recalled messages: Superior lipid profile (PIF10, 73%), flexibility with ARVs (PIF9, 67%), indicated in combo (PIF11, 65%)
Most effective messages: No dose adjustment/renal (PIF3, 62% ME), flexibility (PIF9, 58% ME)
Rep closing improved to 52% (up from prior wave), on par with Dovato, above Biktarvy
~40% of HCPs had improved PIFELTRO perceptions post-interaction

2.3 DELSTRIGO (doravirine/lamivudine/TDF) — Merck
FDA approval: August 2018
Class: NNRTI + 2 NRTIs (3-drug STR)
Positioning: Complete regimen for suppressed patients; being superseded by IDVYNSO as the preferred Merck STR
ZoomRx SFE data: Tracked alongside Pifeltro in multi-product interactions; top message recall covers superior lipid profile (DEL7), appropriate patient types for switch (DEL3), weight change data (DEL8)

3. Competitive Landscape
3.1 Biktarvy (bictegravir/emtricitabine/TAF) — Gilead Sciences
The dominant franchise. Biktarvy generated ~$13B+ globally in 2024, commands ~45-50% share of new prescriptions and switching. DHHS-preferred regimen. 90% sampling penetration per ZoomRx data.
Strengths:

5+ years of real-world data, zero resistance in 9 Phase 3 trials
DHHS guideline-recommended for rapid initiation (only INSTI-based STR with this recommendation)
Physician comfort and familiarity — the "TAGRISSO of HIV"
Gilead's dominant SOV (42%) and highest reach (~80%+ HCPs/month)
Message effectiveness: BIK8 (rapid initiation, 83% ME) and BIK3 (resistance, 81% ME) are the highest-rated messages in the study

Vulnerabilities:

Weight gain signal (INSTI class effect)
Tenofovir (TAF) — renal/bone concerns for long-term use
3-drug regimen (more components than 2-drug alternatives)
Patent cliff concerns (bictegravir composition patent expiry timeline)

3.2 Dovato (dolutegravir/lamivudine) — ViiV Healthcare (GSK)
The 2-drug INSTI alternative. Dovato is IDVYNSO's most direct competitor — both are 2-drug STRs targeting simplification.
Strengths:

Proven 2-drug regimen concept; non-inferior to 3-drug regimens at 144 weeks
PASO DOBLE 96-week data (Oct 2025): matched Biktarvy efficacy with significantly less weight gain
Strong efficacy messaging: DOV1 (viral suppression with fewer medicines) has 80% ME — highest of any message in the study
ViiV's HIV-focused sales force and deep KOL relationships

Vulnerabilities:

INSTI-based (same weight gain class concern, though less than Biktarvy per PASO DOBLE)
Lamivudine backbone (lower resistance barrier than TAF-based regimens)
Dolutegravir patent expiry 2028 (generic competition imminent)

3.3 Cabenuva (cabotegravir + rilpivirine injection) — ViiV Healthcare
The injectable disruptor. Monthly or bimonthly IM injection for virologically suppressed adults.
Strengths:

First long-acting injectable ART — appeals to patients who dislike daily pills
Growing rapidly; projected to represent ~1/3 of GSK's HIV revenue by 2026
Strong patient satisfaction and adherence

Vulnerabilities:

Injection site reactions, requires clinic visits
INSTI-based (cabotegravir)
Not for all patients — oral-preferring patients remain the majority
Patent expiry in US ~2026

3.4 Lenacapavir (Sunlenca) — Gilead Sciences
The next frontier. First-in-class capsid inhibitor. Currently approved for heavily treatment-experienced patients. Being developed as twice-yearly subcutaneous injection for both treatment and PrEP.
Strengths:

Twice-yearly dosing is transformative if broadly approved
Novel MOA with no cross-resistance to existing classes
Gilead's PrEP franchise extension

Threat to Merck: If lenacapavir gains broad treatment approval, it could redefine convenience beyond what daily oral regimens offer. However, it's injectable — a different competitive space from oral simplification.
3.5 Islatravir + Lenacapavir — Merck/Gilead collaboration
Pipeline: Phase 3 trials (ISLEND-1, ISLEND-2) evaluating oral once-weekly islatravir + lenacapavir. This represents Merck's bet on the long-acting oral space and a strategic collaboration with its primary competitor.

4. Merck's HIV Strategy
4.1 Strategic Position
Merck is the #3 player in HIV behind Gilead and ViiV, with a small but growing franchise:

Current portfolio: Pifeltro ($144M peak forecast) and Delstrigo ($369M peak forecast) — combined sub-$500M, not blockbusters
IDVYNSO launch (May 2026): Transformative moment — first product with head-to-head Phase 3 data vs Biktarvy, first non-INSTI 2-drug regimen
Pipeline: DOR/ISL treatment-naive NDA expected (Phase 3 data from CROI 2026 supports filing), islatravir + lenacapavir (once-weekly oral), islatravir + ulonivirine (MK-8507)
Stated commercial opportunity: $15B by mid-2030s (per Merck investor presentation, June 2025)

4.2 Commercial Dynamics (from ZoomRx Studies)
Sales force activity (Aug-Dec 2025):

Merck reps reach ~65% of HCPs monthly, consistently 3rd behind Gilead (~80%) and ViiV (~70%)
Share of voice: Merck 28%, Gilead 42%, ViiV 30%
Activity consistent across IDs vs PCPs, but lower among NP/PAs vs MDs
Average interaction duration: 16.3 minutes (longest in market, above Gilead 15.9, ViiV 14.1)

Messaging strategy:

PIFELTRO messages focus on flexibility/dosing (PIF3, PIF9) and lipid profile (PIF10) — well-prioritized, top recalled messages are also most effective
Patient type focus: PLWH with weight concerns, comorbidities, dyslipidemia, renal impairment — niche but defensible positioning
Competitor contrast: Dovato focuses on efficacy, Biktarvy on legacy/durability — Merck occupies the tolerability/flexibility lane

Rep execution:

General improvement in rep performance this wave across all attributes
iPad/visual aid usage (86% of interactions) is high and correlates with better message recall and product perceptions
Rep closing improved to 52% (vs 41% Biktarvy, on par with 52% Dovato)
Key opportunity: increase NP/PA reach where Gilead/ViiV are stronger

Sampling strategy (July 2025 study):

55% of HCPs prefer sales rep delivery vs 45% mail delivery
Target list HCPs prefer rep delivery 17% more (64% vs 47% off-target)
HIV Specialists prefer rep delivery 12% more (61% vs 49% PCPs)
Biktarvy dominates sampling (90% penetration) with primarily rep delivery
Recommendation for DOR/ISL: Prioritize sales rep delivery, consider hybrid model for high-volume/PCP practices

4.3 Transition from Pifeltro/Delstrigo to IDVYNSO
The 2026 SFE planning document confirms:

DOR/ISL (IDVYNSO) expected approved late April 2026, promotions beginning May 1, 2026
Merck reps will promote ONLY DOR/ISL (no more PIFELTRO promotions)
ZoomRx tracking: 2 waves in 2H 2026 (N=90 unique HCPs per wave), tracking DOR/ISL vs Dovato vs Biktarvy
Study setup post-approval, with 4-6 week buffer before fielding
Wave 1 topline: week of July 27; full report: week of Sep 7
Wave 2 topline: week of Nov 23; full report: week of Jan 11, 2027


5. Key Questions for Vinoth
